mcqs.question: In a doubly linked list, each node contains:

options.text: Only a data part and a next pointer
A data part, a previous pointer, and a next pointer
A data part and two next pointers
Only two pointers without any data

mcqs.explanation: A doubly linked list node contains a data field, a pointer to the previous node, and a pointer to the next node, enabling bidirectional traversal.

mcqs.question: What distinguishes a circular linked list from a linear linked list?

options.text: Circular lists use more memory per node
The last node's next pointer points back to the first node (or head) instead of NULL
Circular lists cannot be traversed completely
Circular lists only exist in doubly linked form

mcqs.explanation: In a circular linked list, the last node's next pointer references the head node, creating a closed loop with no NULL terminator.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the beginning of a singly linked list?

options.text: O(n) because we must traverse to find the position
O(1) - constant time, just update the head pointer
O(log n) - logarithmic time complexity
O(n²) - quadratic time complexity

mcqs.explanation: Insertion at the beginning of a singly linked list is O(1) because it requires only creating a new node and updating the head pointer to point to the new node.

mcqs.question: In a doubly circular linked list with 5 nodes, if you're at node 3 and need to delete it, how many pointer updates are required minimum?

options.text: 1 pointer update
2 pointer updates
3 pointer updates
4 pointer updates

mcqs.explanation: Deleting a node from a doubly circular linked list requires updating only 2 pointers: the next pointer of the previous node and the previous pointer of the next node.

mcqs.question: A music playlist application needs to continuously play songs in a loop and allow users to navigate to the previous song efficiently. Which linked list implementation is most suitable?

options.text: Singly linked list
Doubly linked list
Array-based implementation
Stack-based implementation

mcqs.explanation: A doubly linked list allows both forward and backward navigation in O(1) time, and when made circular, it enables seamless looping from last song back to first.

mcqs.question: In a singly circular linked list, which operation becomes more efficient compared to its linear counterpart?

options.text: Deletion at the end of the list
Finding the middle element
Traversing all elements starting from any node
Random access by index

mcqs.explanation: In a circular linked list, you can start traversal from any node and visit all elements without checking for NULL, making complete traversal flexible.

mcqs.question: Consider implementing a round-robin CPU scheduler using a linked list. Each process gets CPU time in turn, then moves to the back. Which data structure provides the most natural implementation with O(1) time for the 'move-to-back' operation?

options.text: Singly linked list with tail pointer
Doubly linked list without tail pointer
Circular singly linked list with tail pointer
Linear doubly linked list with head pointer only

mcqs.explanation: A circular singly linked list with a tail pointer allows O(1) implementation of round-robin scheduling: the tail's next points to the next process, and moving a process to the back just updates one pointer.

mcqs.question: You are given a singly linked list and need to check whether it forms a palindrome (reads same forwards and backwards). What is the most space-efficient approach and its complexity?

options.text: Reverse the entire list in place - O(1) space, O(n) time
Use a stack to store first half - O(n) space, O(n) time
Recursively compare from both ends - O(n) stack space, O(n) time
Use a hash table - O(n) space, O(n) time

mcqs.explanation: Reversing the first half of the list in place and comparing with the second half achieves O(1) auxiliary space (excluding the list itself) and O(n) time, making it the most space-efficient approach.
